chapter_title: 医薬品とドラッグデリバリー
synopsis: This chapter applies Hansen Solubility Parameters to pharmaceutical formulation and drug delivery, covering drug solubility classification via RED, transdermal permeation enhancer selection using a three-component drug-skin-enhancer HSP system, liposome membrane permeability prediction, nanoparticle drug loading optimization, cocrystal screening for API-coformer pairs, polymorph and solvate risk assessment in the RED band 0.5-1.5, carrier excipient selection, and inhalation drug-propellant compatibility. The HSP framework provides a unified thermodynamic basis for rational formulation design, reducing the need for extensive experimental screening in each of these pharmaceutical applications.
key_concepts: Drug solubility classification using RED (Relative Energy Difference) in HSP space
HSP sphere construction for active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) from solubility data
Transdermal drug delivery: three-component HSP system (drug-skin-enhancer)
Chemical permeation enhancer (CPE) selection via HSP distance minimization to skin lipid domain
Liposome bilayer permeability prediction from drug-phospholipid HSP affinity
Nanoparticle drug loading: polymer-drug HSP matching for encapsulation efficiency optimization
Cocrystal screening: API-coformer HSP distance as predictor of cocrystal formation likelihood
Polymorph and solvate risk assessment in the RED transition zone (0.5-1.5)
Carrier excipient selection for solid dispersions using HSP miscibility criteria
Inhalation drug formulation: propellant-drug HSP compatibility for metered-dose inhalers (MDIs)
Biopharmaceutics Classification System (BCS) correlation with HSP solubility predictions
Amorphous solid dispersion (ASD) stability prediction from polymer-drug HSP distance
Hansen solubility sphere methodology applied to pharmaceutical solvent screening
Supersaturation and nucleation tendency related to solvent HSP proximity to API

equations.name: HSP distance (Ra) for drug-solvent pair
equations.explanation: Hansen distance between a drug molecule and a solvent in 3D HSP space, used to predict drug solubility; smaller Ra indicates higher expected solubility

equations.name: Relative Energy Difference (RED) for drug solubility classification
equations.explanation: RED ratio classifying drug-solvent compatibility: RED < 1 indicates likely solubility (good solvent), RED > 1 indicates likely insolubility, and RED = 1 is the boundary of the solubility sphere

equations.name: Flory-Huggins interaction parameter for drug-polymer compatibility
equations.explanation: Estimation of the Flory-Huggins chi parameter for drug-polymer pairs, critical for predicting miscibility in amorphous solid dispersions and nanoparticle drug loading

equations.name: Transdermal flux enhancement factor
equations.explanation: Semi-empirical relationship linking the flux enhancement factor to HSP distances among the drug, skin lipid barrier, and chemical permeation enhancer in a three-component system [uncertain]

equations.name: Drug loading capacity in nanoparticles
equations.explanation: Drug loading capacity of a polymeric nanoparticle scales inversely with the squared HSP distance between drug and carrier polymer, reflecting thermodynamic miscibility [uncertain]

equations.name: Cocrystal formation likelihood from HSP
equations.explanation: Partial solubility parameter distance between API and coformer; empirically, cocrystal formation is more likely when this distance falls within an intermediate range, indicating complementary but not identical intermolecular interactions

equations.name: Polymorph/solvate risk zone criterion
equations.explanation: Solvents with RED values in the transition zone (0.5 to 1.5) relative to the API present elevated risk of forming solvates or inducing polymorphic transformations during crystallization

historical_context: The application of solubility parameters to pharmaceutical systems traces back to Hildebrand's original solubility parameter and early work by Hancock et al. (1997) on predicting drug solubility in excipient matrices. Hansen's three-component extension was adopted for pharmaceutical use notably by Archer (1992) and later systematized by Gharagheizi (2008) and Mohammad et al. (2011). Transdermal enhancer selection using HSP was explored by Fang et al. (2003) and Minghetti et al. (2007), who demonstrated the three-component drug-skin-enhancer approach. Abbott and Hansen (2008) formalized the HSP sphere methodology for pharmaceutical solvent selection and emphasized the RED classification for drug solubility screening. Cocrystal screening using HSP was advanced by Mohammad et al. (2011) and Greenhalgh et al. (1999), who showed that HSP distance between API and coformer correlates with cocrystal formation propensity. The polymorph/solvate risk zone concept (RED 0.5-1.5) derives from observations that partial solubility creates conditions favoring crystal form diversity, as discussed by Threlfall (2000) and Bernstein (2002). Nanoparticle drug loading optimization via HSP matching emerged from work by Letchford and Burt (2007) and was further developed by Duong et al. (2015). The field continues to evolve with computational HSP prediction methods enabling virtual screening of drug formulation candidates.
learning_objectives: Apply the RED classification to predict drug solubility in pharmaceutical solvents and rank candidate solvents for API dissolution
Design a transdermal drug delivery formulation by selecting chemical permeation enhancers using three-component HSP analysis (drug-skin-enhancer)
Predict nanoparticle drug loading efficiency from HSP matching between the drug and carrier polymer, and optimize encapsulation
Screen API-coformer pairs for cocrystal formation potential using HSP distance criteria and identify promising candidates
Assess polymorph and solvate risk during crystallization by identifying solvents in the RED transition zone (0.5-1.5)
practical_applications: Pharmaceutical solvent screening for drug purification and crystallization using HSP-based RED classification
Selection of chemical permeation enhancers for transdermal drug delivery patches
Formulation of polymeric nanoparticles with maximized drug loading through HSP-guided polymer selection
Cocrystal engineering to improve aqueous solubility and bioavailability of poorly soluble BCS Class II drugs
Polymorph risk assessment during process development to avoid undesired crystal form changes
Carrier and excipient selection for amorphous solid dispersions (ASDs) with long-term stability
Propellant compatibility screening for metered-dose inhaler (MDI) formulations
Liposome formulation optimization for encapsulation of hydrophobic and amphiphilic drugs

worked_examples.title: Drug solubility screening for ibuprofen in 20 common solvents
worked_examples.description: Determine the HSP sphere of ibuprofen (dD=17.4, dP=4.0, dH=9.6, R0=10.2) from experimental solubility data. Calculate Ra and RED for 20 pharmaceutical solvents, classify each as good/partial/poor solvent, and compare predictions with experimental solubility values to validate the HSP sphere.

worked_examples.title: Transdermal enhancer selection for caffeine delivery
worked_examples.description: Using HSP values for caffeine (drug), stratum corneum lipid domain (skin), and five candidate enhancers (oleic acid, propylene glycol, DMSO, Azone, ethanol), compute the three-component HSP distances. Rank enhancers by predicted flux enhancement and identify the optimal enhancer or binary enhancer combination.

worked_examples.title: Cocrystal screening for carbamazepine
worked_examples.description: Calculate HSP distances between carbamazepine (API) and 10 candidate coformers (saccharin, nicotinamide, glutaric acid, succinic acid, etc.). Apply the empirical HSP distance criterion for cocrystal formation likelihood. Compare predictions with known experimental cocrystal data from the Cambridge Structural Database.

worked_examples.title: Polymorph risk assessment for paracetamol crystallization
worked_examples.description: Compute RED values for paracetamol in 15 crystallization solvents. Identify solvents in the RED 0.5-1.5 risk zone and correlate with known polymorphic outcomes (Form I vs Form II). Recommend a solvent system that minimizes solvate and polymorph risk.

figures_needed.title: Drug Solubility Sphere in HSP Space
figures_needed.description: 3D Hansen space plot showing the solubility sphere of a model drug (e.g., ibuprofen) with good solvents plotted inside the sphere and poor solvents outside, illustrating RED-based classification

figures_needed.title: Three-Component Transdermal HSP Diagram
figures_needed.description: 3D HSP plot showing the drug, skin lipid domain, and multiple candidate permeation enhancers, with arrows indicating the enhancer's ability to bridge the drug-skin HSP gap

figures_needed.title: Nanoparticle Drug Loading vs HSP Distance
figures_needed.description: Scatter plot of experimental drug loading capacity (%) versus Ra between drug and carrier polymer for multiple drug-polymer pairs, showing the inverse correlation

figures_needed.title: Cocrystal Formation Map in HSP Space
figures_needed.description: 2D projection of HSP space showing an API at the center with coformers plotted by HSP distance; successful cocrystals marked distinctly from failed attempts, revealing the optimal distance range

figures_needed.title: Polymorph/Solvate Risk Zone Diagram
figures_needed.description: Radial diagram centered on an API showing concentric RED zones (0-0.5 dissolution zone, 0.5-1.5 risk zone, >1.5 safe zone) with crystallization solvents plotted and color-coded by observed polymorphic outcome

figures_needed.title: Amorphous Solid Dispersion Stability Map
figures_needed.description: Plot of drug-polymer HSP distance versus physical stability (time to crystallization onset) for multiple ASD formulations, demonstrating the stability advantage of HSP-matched systems

figures_needed.title: Liposome Permeability vs Drug HSP
figures_needed.description: Plot showing drug permeability through liposome bilayers as a function of drug-phospholipid HSP distance, illustrating the optimal range for encapsulation and controlled release

figures_needed.title: MDI Propellant-Drug Compatibility Chart
figures_needed.description: Bar chart comparing HSP distances between common inhaled drugs and propellants (HFA-134a, HFA-227ea), with solubility/suspension stability outcomes annotated

tables_needed.title: HSP Values for Common Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients
tables_needed.description: Table of deltaD, deltaP, deltaH, and R0 values for 15-20 common drugs including ibuprofen, paracetamol, carbamazepine, indomethacin, caffeine, aspirin, and others

tables_needed.title: RED Classification for Drug-Solvent Pairs
tables_needed.description: Matrix of RED values for 5 model drugs against 15 pharmaceutical solvents with solubility classification (good/partial/poor)

tables_needed.title: Chemical Permeation Enhancer HSP Values
tables_needed.description: Table listing CPEs (oleic acid, DMSO, Azone, terpenes, fatty acids, propylene glycol, etc.) with HSP values and Ra to stratum corneum lipid domain

tables_needed.title: Cocrystal Formation: HSP Distance vs Experimental Outcome
tables_needed.description: Table comparing computed API-coformer HSP distances with experimental cocrystal screening results (yes/no) for 20+ API-coformer pairs

tables_needed.title: Polymer Carriers for Nanoparticle Drug Loading
tables_needed.description: Table of common pharmaceutical polymers (PLGA, PLA, PCL, PEG-PLA, chitosan) with HSP values, and drug loading data for model drugs

tables_needed.title: Polymorph/Solvate Risk Classification by Solvent RED
tables_needed.description: Table mapping crystallization solvents to RED values for a model API, with observed crystal forms and solvate occurrence annotated

tables_needed.title: Propellant HSP Values and Drug Compatibility
tables_needed.description: Table of HFA propellants and legacy CFC propellants with HSP values, and compatibility ratings for common inhaled drug substances

software_demo.tab: Drug Solubility (薬物溶解性) tab, Dispersant Selection (分散剤選定) tab for transdermal enhancer mode, and Cocrystal Screening (共結晶スクリーニング) tab
software_demo.inputs: In the Drug Solubility tab, enter HSP values for a target drug (e.g., ibuprofen: dD=17.4, dP=4.0, dH=9.6) and R0. In the Dispersant Selection tab, enter the three-component system: drug HSP, skin HSP (dD=16.0, dP=3.5, dH=6.0), and candidate enhancer HSPs. In the Cocrystal Screening tab, enter the API HSP and a list of candidate coformer HSP values.
software_demo.expected_output: The Drug Solubility tab displays RED values for each solvent, ranked from best to worst, with color-coded classification. The Dispersant Selection tab ranks enhancers by predicted transdermal enhancement. The Cocrystal Screening tab shows API-coformer HSP distances with cocrystal formation likelihood scores.
software_demo.workflow: 1. Open the Drug Solubility tab and enter ibuprofen HSP values. 2. Review the ranked solvent list and verify against known solubility data. 3. Switch to the Dispersant Selection tab and configure a transdermal system with caffeine as drug and stratum corneum as target. 4. Evaluate enhancer candidates and select the top-ranked option. 5. Open the Cocrystal Screening tab and enter carbamazepine with 10 coformer candidates to identify promising cocrystal pairs.

review_questions: Explain how the RED classification system is applied to predict drug solubility in pharmaceutical solvents. What are the limitations of using a single sphere to represent drug solubility behavior, especially for drugs with multiple functional groups?

Describe the three-component HSP framework for transdermal permeation enhancer selection. How does the enhancer modify the HSP distance between the drug and the skin lipid barrier, and what practical considerations beyond HSP affect transdermal delivery?

Why does the RED zone of 0.5-1.5 present elevated polymorph and solvate risk during crystallization? Propose a crystallization development strategy that uses HSP analysis to minimize the risk of undesired crystal forms.

Compare the HSP-based approach to cocrystal screening with other computational methods (e.g., COSMO-RS, molecular complementarity). What are the advantages and limitations of using HSP distances to predict API-coformer cocrystal formation?

Discuss how HSP matching between drug and polymer carrier influences nanoparticle drug loading efficiency and release kinetics. What role does the chi parameter play in predicting long-term stability of drug-loaded nanoparticles?
